sentences.text: Yeah.
First of all, we look at them like crazy.
They walked in, they marched in, the whole army, men and women.
And we saw them going under the showers together.
We were looking at it that we couldn't believe it.
And then after they cleaned up, they decide-- luckily I had a friend over there.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] She's going to tell them to stop.
But who's going to let her in?
But, so-- so anyway, good I had a friend, a man who was taking care of me and my mother.
He was much older.
Somehow he was like our-- what shall I say?
I was taking care of him.
So he makes-- so when they came, they have to washed up, they start raping women.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And women were jumping, hiding, you can't imagine.
But luckily, we had him.
And he hid us in someplace and he protect us from it.
Yeah.
So we weren't affected.
But they were raping women right and left who could hardly walk, who were sick, who were from concentration camp.
And then they wanted us to work in the hospitals with the wounded soldiers.
So you know, always some people become like leaders.
They said, go to town, take the Germans.
What do-- these women can hardly walk, can hardly breathe.
And they came hungry.
And they emptied out all the warehouses, whatever food was there.
We had nothing to eat, absolutely nothing.
So they said go to town, knock on the doors, and that's what we did.
In groups like that, and walked from door to door, begging for a potato, or piece of bread.
And that Czechoslovakians are very good people, very good natured people.
And they tried to help us.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And-- oh, here he is.
Very good natured people.
And tried to-- to help-- tried to help us.
And-- and we somehow survived on begging people for bread.
That's the--
Yeah.
Yeah.
Then the Russians took over the camp, and-- and the guy-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the guy-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they took over the camp, the Russian, and he managed the camp.
And he formed the transport of going to Poland.
[SIGHS] Maybe a few weeks.
And when we used to go out feeling free to walk in the streets, we really-- you know, like a bird in his cage for years in a cage and doesn't know what to do?
We started telling each other, don't hold you hands in the pocket, that's not nice.
Don't whistle.
We didn't know how to behave.
We really didn't-- don't forget, we were locked up for six years.
All of sudden we are free.
So one was telling the other one, you don't do this, you don't do that.
And so on.
And then the camp director formed a transport, whoever wanted.
Not everybody went back to Poland.
Whoever wants to go to Poland they can.
And other people went other places.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we went to Poland, like-- What sort of transport was this?
How did you travel there?
Also in cattle cars, if I'm not mistaken.
I don't remember, but by train.
And I think took two weeks to travel from Czechoslovakia to Krakow.
It took a very long time.
Nah.
No, we were free.
It was different.
So we went to Poland.
And that's where we plan to reunited with our families.
So when we arrived at the station in Krakow, right away we were greeted by Polacks.
Still so many of you are left?
That was the greeting to us.
We thought they killed you all.
Because they were very instrumental helping Germans in killing Jews.
Germans didn't recognize a Jew as easily as a Polack.
You could have passed, finagled your way to save yourself.
But the Poles spotted a Jew immediately.
And they helped the Germans a lot.
Otherwise, more people would survive.
Poles were-- played a very big part in it.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So the Jews who were coming from concentration camp, we had two houses, a Jewish dormitory and the orphanage, which was from before the war.
So they put [? up ?] people in those houses, an orphanage and the Jewish dormitory.
We were put into the Jewish dormitory.
Do how many people were there?
In one room-- not of this size, a regular bedroom size-- 10 people.
You slept on the floor, wherever you could.
As a matter of fact, my uncle came back from concentration camp.
And I had a spot on the floor so I gave him my spot and I slept on the table.
I don't think we had good conditions or anything like that.
And so we came to Poland to wait for my father.
And every day you ran to the Jewish commit-- there was a Jewish committee set up, which they had lists who survived.
Because people register from all concentration camps.
And that's what it was, our language, communication.
And this way you found out if somebody is alive, who is alive, who is dead.
So from our family when seven people, seven men, and my uncle was the oldest, he came back.
He came back.
But the rest, and he told me about my father, that he was killed in February just before the war.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Just before the war he was killed in February.
Yeah.
Yeah.
May ended, and February he got killed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we stayed in Poland.
The Poles didn't make it too comfortable for us.
And we didn't know what to do.
And then they made up our mind.
There was a pogrom, a big pogrom in Krakow, which they-- I had the leaflet.
But it dried out, fell apart, throw down leaflets that Jews came from concentration camp, they're under nourished, they have no blood.
So they killing our children to take the blood from them.
And they-- where did they attack?
Just the dormitory and the orphanage home, because there were the Jews.
We had to bribe Russian police to manage they should protect us.
Next morning, 5 o'clock in the morning, we left for the border.
This was in August, right?
September.
September 9th.
This, I don't remember.
But I wasn't in the dormitory.
I was in a different part of Krakow.
And I was going home.
And I see what's going on.
So I was standing with a group, with a mob.
I feel, I'll hide.
And all of a sudden, they turn around.
They said there is a Jew.
And I run away.
And I don't know how I managed to get into the building not to be killed.
They were hitting, stones, and everything.
So we managed to run away.
A lot of Jewish soldiers, who were in the Russian army, they threw down their uniforms in the toilets and changed to regular clothes.
And we crossed the border to Czechoslovakia, to Prague.
Who were you were with at this time?
With my mother, my sister.
No.
My uncle remained because he was waiting for other family-- members of family to find out what's going on.
And-- and in Czechoslovakia we went as Greek Jews.
They smuggled us out to Germany.
The Jewish committee, as Greek Jews.
And we lived in Landsberg in Germany.
DP camp, yes.
You see, once we were in the DP camps, we tried to immigrate someplace.
And nobody wanted us.
And my mother had family in Belgium, a brother who remained alive, and-- and a sister.
So they-- you know, the mail used to-- to go two months a letter used to go.
But they said come to Brussels.
Smuggle through the borders and come to Brussels in Belgium.
So we tried to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] smuggle through the border.
We left Landsberg.
We went to Hanover and then to Aachen.
How long were you in Landsberg for?
We came in October, I think.
Maybe a year, year and a half.
I don't know.
In Landsberg while I was-- I was learning a trade to become a designer, pattern maker, whatever you call it.
So I was going to school.
And I was getting extra portion of bread, too, for it.
So-- but we couldn't cross the border for no money in the world.
You see, maybe if the men was with us, it would have been different.
So we went back to Hanover.
My uncle came from Belgium to Hanover.
And we stayed in Hanover, privately we lived.
And then we heard there is Truman opened the door for Jewish displaced people.
So we then went back to Landsberg to register to go to United States.
You see, when the war ended, for us the war didn't ended.
We were wandering.
People didn't want us any place.
It was-- they weren't killing us anymore, except in Poland.
They weren't killing us.
But we were struggling, constantly wandering.
We didn't know where we gonna wind up.
Who's gonna let us in?
And so on.
But once Truman opened the door, we registered.
And we left Germany in 1947.
We arrived here January '48.
How did you get here?
By boat.
How long the passage?
Was two weeks, like Chris Columbus.
Because I was so sick.
The minute I got on that ship, on that ship, I was throwing up.
I was sick.
So I says, what took him so long so far away to discovering America?
[LAUGHS] And when I came to this country I was 18 years old.
January 28th, 1948.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] I felt like kissing the ground.
Because if you want to work, you could make a life, if you're not lazy.
Not that I had any trades, any schooling, or anything.
But still, you got together with other survivors.
So they gave me a job.
I had eight jobs within two months because I was anxious to make money.
No.
No English whatsoever.
And I didn't-- I had no trade, no nothing.
But I went from job to job, and I was learning more.
My dream was to have six pairs underwear, because I had only two.
And then I'm writing a letter to my girlfriend in Germany.
Just says, I am working for an underwear factory.
[LAUGHS] So she answers me, maybe now you'll get your six pairs underwear.
And I went from job to job.
On each job I learned something else.
My sister was the baby.
She didn't do nothing.
And my mother in the real life, she was the princess.
She never worked.
So-- and I couldn't see my mother should go to work.
My mother who was-- had the life of [?
Riley, ?] breakfast in bed before the war, I couldn't picture.
And she was-- you know, now I'm thinking back, she was only 43 or 44 years old.
But I would never let her go to work.
And I carried the whole burden.
Oh yeah, sure.
I lived with my mother, my sister, and my responsibility.
Because my father said to me before we separated, take care of your mother and your sister.
So this was always in back of my mind.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It wasn't easy.
Believe me.
I worked like a dog in factories until one guy felt sorry for me.
He says to me, little girl, I'm going to teach you a trade.
But at the beginning you won't make any money.
The minimum salary was $25 a week.
He says you'll make 50, but I have high hopes on you.
And that's what happened.
He taught me a trade.
I became a lining maker.
In no time I was making $60, $80 a week.
But they were only piecework.
Only piecework, because I was very fast.
And when there was a season, they loved me.
When the season was off, they criticized me that my seams aren't even on the machine.
[LAUGHS] But they never laid me off.
They kept me.
And then I went at night to school.
I went five nights to school.
After working in factory, the work in factory to produce so much money-- the heat, no air conditions, no fan.
I used to wear a house dress with a wet handkerchief on my neck.
And the machine was running like fire.
I went to school five nights a week.
And I went to Rosewell High School.
I used to fall asleep many times in the school.
And I used to do my homework on the subways.
But I accomplished, because I didn't wanna work in the factory.
To me, this was something great that you can't imagine.
Still the old upbringing was left in me.
I couldn't be faced with common people, with their rough talk, with rough language, which you heard in concentration camp, plenty [LAUGHS] of it.
But I couldn't stand it.
And then I got married.
I was married previously, which my son is from my first marriage.
But--
How old? 21.
Yeah.
And then 1953, I married my husband.
The first time I was married-- I got married in July.
And when I was pregnant we separated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
But then my husband married me.
And he adopted my son.
18 months.
And then a year later my daughter was born.
Oh, yeah.
Plenty, especially when the children were small.
And I always dreamt that the Germans going to take them away.
I was hiding them.
There's-- they're going to kill them.
This was a dream which was haunting me all the time while the kids were small.
To my children?
No.
I never talked to them.
I-- I hide it in a way.
I never advertised.
I wanted to become Americanized in the worst way.
I wanted to learn the language in a hurry.
And which came to me very hard time, not good in languages.
I thought I'll never speak English.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- I read all the literature here when my son was little.
I tried to educate myself in certain way, because to my father always was important.
He says, read, reading is the key to the knowledge.
And I did this here on my own.
I did lot of reading, I have to admit.
And where was I?
What happened to your sister?
My sister was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hurt by the war.
She was much younger than me.
And she was beaten in Plaszow plenty.
And I never knew that the beating did any damage to her.
I consider her the baby.
The youngest sister, the baby, she should be taken care of.
Meanwhile, she got the brain damage.
When we came here it showed up.
She start having very heavy epilepsy attacks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and she used to fall.
She used to get hurt so much.
And she broke so many bones and everything, and her head was hit plenty times.
That's-- she-- I would say she's more slow.
OK, she grew up in concentration camp.
She didn't see anything.
Maybe it would be different.
I don't know.
It's-- she's slow.
And she's in the institution since we are-- since I'm here.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She still speaks Polish.
I would say till five years ago she had two grades of education, no more.
She still wrote to me in Polish letters.
But like a second grade child, you know, baby.
Now she can't.
As a matter of fact I saw her a month-- no-- in January for her birthday, because it was her birthday.
So she says to me she had fantastic memory and good in math.
That is unbelievable.
She knew exactly what date we were there, how many days are left since we saw her.
So she says to me-- she calls me in Polish-- [NON-ENGLISH], I don't remember how old I am now.
Could you tell me?
She remembered everybody's birthday.
My grandchildren and children.
But I think the medication has a lot to do.
What she's taking, or whatever the agent.
So she says, you know, my head doesn't work anymore, so good.
Could you tell me how old am I now?
This was a very depressing thing.
How do you think that the experiences you went through influenced the way you've raised your children?
Experiences-- I tell you, when I had my children, I didn't know what to do.
I was never exposed to small children.
I wasn't exposed to a family life, if you want to think about that logically.
From the age of 10 to 16 I was locked up.
What was my upbringing?
Murder, fear.
And I was afraid that I'll make a bad mother.
I was so scared that you can't imagine.
Then I started reading Dr.
Spock book.
He was my guidance.
[LAUGHS] He was my god.
And my kids couldn't swear till today probably.
[LAUGHS] I never hollered at them.
I never-- I never lost my temper.
I used to always sit and talk, discuss what was something wrong.
And I have to say, I have two wonderful children.
Really, two nice kids.
I-- I would punish them.
I said, I will take away from you this and that, because you shouldn't be doing, but I never raised my hand.
I never lost my temper, which people couldn't understand, especially years ago you had the windows open, you had no air conditions.
So supper time you heard everyone screaming at their kids.
My neighbors used to say, how come you never scream?
[LAUGHS] I say, I don't have-- what about?
And I always sat and talked with them.
And Dr.
Spock was my guidance.
You Know, I didn't know, which people take for granted things.
Like my best girlfriend had a baby.
I was earning nice living later on when I learned the trade and everything, that you go visiting someone that has a new baby, you have to bring a gift.
I didn't know about that.
So many things what you take for granted which comes natural to people, we didn't know about it.
I personally didn't know about it.
Later on when the boy got big-- this was the first baby I ever held in my arms-- I used to say, I was so stupid.
I could have afford, but I didn't know that you have to do it.
And so many things that people take for granted.
Like I remember I went to work in the office.
I called my girlfriend, I said, how do I use a staple remover?
Which to every child, it's normal because they go to school.
They-- they work in the school offices.
They helping out the teachers.
I didn't have all that.
I was deprived of everything.
Whatever I did, I did by force really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to learn, but we were busy.
Not only six years, I would say eight years out of our life, living in the displaced camps, going through the pogroms, and even coming to United States where it's a free country.
But we struggled till we came to something.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Which really people take things so much-- so many things might look stupid, she doesn't know that.
And the friend of mine always laughed because I worked myself to a very nice position.
So she says the-- the one who calls me doesn't know how to use a staple remover, look where she is now.
[LAUGHS]
